questions.prompt: Gradient clipping (rescaling g when ‖g‖ > τ) addresses exploding gradients in RNNs. Why does it NOT fix the vanishing gradient problem?

options.text: Clipping is only applied during inference, not training
Clipping rescales large gradients downward but cannot amplify gradients that are already near zero
Vanishing gradients only occur in feedforward networks, not RNNs
Clipping changes the direction of the gradient, which prevents useful learning

questions.explanation: Clipping is a ceiling operation — it prevents gradient norms from growing beyond τ but does nothing when gradients are already tiny. Vanishing gradients require architectural solutions (LSTM gating, residual connections) that create paths where gradients can flow without multiplying through W_h at every step.
questions.hint: Think about what clipping does to a gradient that is already very small (e.g., 1e-8).

questions.prompt: In an LSTM, the cell state update c_t = f_t ⊙ c_{t-1} + i_t ⊙ c̃_t provides a 'gradient highway'. What makes this gradient path qualitatively different from a vanilla RNN's gradient path?

options.text: The cell state uses tanh instead of sigmoid, which has better gradient properties
Gradients flow through element-wise multiplication by gate values, not matrix multiplication by W_h
The cell state is normalized by layer normalization at each step
The forget gate is initialized to 1, so gradients are always preserved

questions.explanation: In a vanilla RNN, ∂h_{t+1}/∂h_t involves multiplication by W_h (full matrix). In the LSTM cell path, ∂c_t/∂c_{t-1} = f_t — just element-wise multiplication by the forget gate. If f_t ≈ 1, the gradient passes through nearly unchanged across many steps.
questions.hint: Compare ∂h_{t+1}/∂h_t in a vanilla RNN to ∂c_t/∂c_{t-1} in an LSTM.

questions.prompt: Scaled dot-product attention divides QK^T by √d_k before the softmax. Why is this scaling necessary?

options.text: To ensure attention weights sum to 1 across all query positions
To prevent the values V from dominating the attention output
Because dot products grow in variance with d_k, pushing softmax into near-zero gradient regions
To match the learning rate schedule used in transformer training

questions.explanation: If q_i and k_i are independent with mean 0 and variance 1, their dot product q·k = Σq_i·k_i has variance d_k. Large dot products drive softmax into saturation where one weight ≈ 1 and all others ≈ 0, producing near-zero gradients. Dividing by √d_k restores unit variance regardless of d_k.
questions.hint: Consider what happens to the softmax when d_k is large and dot products are proportionally large.

questions.prompt: A self-attention layer over a sequence of length n has what memory complexity?

options.text: O(n) — linear in sequence length
O(n log n) — due to the softmax normalization
O(n²) — the full attention matrix must be materialized
O(n · d_model) — one vector per token

questions.explanation: The attention matrix QK^T ∈ ℝ^{n×n} must be computed and stored before multiplying by V. This O(n²) memory cost is the primary bottleneck for long-context transformers — addressed by FlashAttention (kernel fusion to avoid materializing the full matrix) and sparse attention patterns.
questions.hint: The attention score matrix has one entry for every (query, key) pair.

questions.prompt: Which positional encoding scheme naturally encodes *relative* position in the attention score — making attention between positions m and n depend only on m−n — enabling better length generalization?

options.text: Sinusoidal absolute encodings (Vaswani et al. 2017)
Learned absolute position embeddings (BERT, GPT-2)
Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE, used in LLaMA)
No positional encoding — transformers are permutation-equivariant by design

questions.explanation: RoPE rotates the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to their absolute position. The dot product q_m · k_n then depends only on the relative offset (n−m), making position-dependent attention patterns naturally transferable to longer sequences. LLaMA, Mistral, and most modern decoder LLMs use RoPE.
questions.hint: The key property is that only the *relative* offset between positions affects the attention score.

questions.prompt: Complete the causal (autoregressive) mask for scaled dot-product attention. Scores where future tokens would be attended to should be set to negative infinity before the softmax:
questions.codeTemplate:
import torch
# scores: (B, H, T, T) — raw attention logits
T = scores.size(-1)
mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, device=scores.device), diagonal=1).bool()
scores = scores.masked_fill(___, float('-inf'))
weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
questions.explanation: torch.triu with diagonal=1 marks the upper triangle (future positions) as True. masked_fill replaces those positions with -inf, making softmax assign exactly 0 weight to future tokens — enforcing the autoregressive constraint.
questions.hint: mask is already a boolean tensor where True marks positions to fill with -inf.
